title: Applying polyurethane varnish to bare wood furniture
subtitle: Apply a hard-wearing polyurethane varnish to an unfinished or stripped timber surface
excerpt: How to apply an oil-based or water-based polyurethane varnish to bare or stripped wood furniture for a durable protective finish. Covers raising the grain, applying coats with light sanding between, and avoiding the most common brush and lap mark problems.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6150: Code of practice for painting of buildings. Traditional furniture finishing practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: The controlled raising of wood fibres by dampening bare timber with water before the first coat of a water-based finish. Water causes the fibres to swell slightly; once dry, they are sanded flat with fine-grit paper. If the grain is not pre-raised, the first coat of water-based varnish will raise the grain as it dries, leaving a rough, hairy surface that requires more sanding. Raising the grain deliberately and sanding before varnishing avoids this problem.

A visible ridge or line in a varnished surface caused by brushing over an area where the varnish has already begun to set on the film edges. Avoiding lap marks requires working quickly across a panel in continuous strokes without stopping and restarting on a partially-cured edge. Work in manageable sections and always pick up from the wet edge of the previous pass.

content.text: Polyurethane varnish dries to a hard, cross-linked film that resists water, heat, and abrasion. It is the standard protective finish for table tops, wooden floors, and utility furniture. Applied correctly (thin coats, light sanding between) it builds a clear, even film in three coats. Applied incorrectly (thick coats, lap marks, poor sanding) it looks plastic and uneven. The aim is a film thin enough to be transparent but thick enough to protect.

Polyurethane varnish (oil-based dries harder; water-based dries faster and is easier to clean up; choose based on drying time available and finish preference). A fine-bristle brush (25 mm for small pieces, 50 mm for large panels; clean bristles are essential). 120-grit, 240-grit, and 320-grit sanding paper. A clean cloth for wiping dust. Mineral spirits (white spirit) for oil-based; water for water-based brush cleaning.
Sand the bare wood to 120-grit with the grain, then to 180-grit. Remove all dust with a clean cloth. For water-based varnish,

: wipe the surface lightly with a damp cloth, allow to dry for 30 minutes, then sand with 180-grit to knock back the raised fibres. Wipe away the sanding dust. The surface is now ready for the first coat without the grain disruption that would otherwise happen with the first coat of water-based varnish.

Thin the first coat slightly (10% mineral spirit for oil-based; 10% water for water-based); a thinned first coat penetrates the wood more deeply and bonds better than a full-strength coat. Apply with the grain in long, even strokes. Work quickly to avoid

. Do not re-brush over areas already coated: once the varnish has been applied, leave it; dragging a brush through partially-cured varnish creates streaks. Allow to cure fully (4 to 6 hours for water-based; overnight for oil-based).
Step 3, sand and apply further coats

Sand the dried first coat lightly with 240-grit to knock back any dust particles or grain raised by the varnish. Wipe off dust. Apply the second coat full-strength. Allow to cure. Sand with 320-grit. Apply the third coat. Three coats is the standard: two coats protect adequately; three coats are needed on a table top or any surface exposed to hard use.

Gloss polyurethane gives the hardest film. Matte and satin versions contain matting agents that reduce hardness slightly. Use the matte or satin finish on the final coat only. The intermediate coats can be gloss regardless of the final finish. Only the last coat's sheen is visible. A matte final coat over gloss intermediate coats gives maximum durability with a low sheen.

attrs.symptom: Varnish drying with a milky or cloudy appearance
attrs.cause: Water-based varnish applied in a cold or damp room (below 10°C), or over a damp surface.
attrs.fix: Allow to cure fully in a warm room. The cloudiness often clears as the film continues to cure. On new work, apply water-based varnish only in rooms above 15°C with low humidity.

attrs.symptom: Brush marks visible in the cured film
attrs.cause: Varnish applied too thick or brush dragged at the wrong angle.
attrs.fix: Sand flat with 240-grit, removing the brush marks to a flat surface. Apply the next coat thinned by 10%, using a high-quality bristle brush and long, even strokes parallel to the grain.
